title: What Humanity Needs To Flourish In The Next Decade | NOEMA
gist: AI capability is outpacing the institutions meant to govern and absorb it, and the authors argue that 'reverse alignment' — deliberate large-scale investment in identity, privacy, provenance, data markets, democracy, labor and education infrastructure — is a prerequisite (not a supplement) for AI to deliver broad prosperity rather than 'productivity without prosperity, execution without verification and capacity without constraint.'

insights.claim: For every dollar spent making AI systems more capable, only a tiny fraction is spent on the institutional, educational, organizational and civic infrastructure needed to integrate those systems into society safely and productively.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Flags a massive underfunded category — 'sociotechnical' infrastructure (credentialing, provenance, workforce transition) — as the next investment and policy frontier as capex floods into raw capability.
insights.quote: For every dollar spent to make AI systems more capable, a tiny fraction goes toward the institutional, educational, organizational and civic infrastructure needed to integrate these systems into society productively and safely.
insights.locator: The imbalance manifests in the flow of capital

insights.claim: AI alignment is a two-sided problem: even a perfectly aligned system will underdeliver or cause serious harm if deployed into democracies, economies and legal systems that are unprepared to receive it.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Reframes 'AI safety' to include societal readiness, expanding the addressable problem set beyond model-level alignment work.
insights.quote: Even a perfectly aligned system will underdeliver on its potential or cause serious harm if it is deployed into democracies, economies and legal systems unprepared to receive it.

insights.claim: The scope of institutional adaptation required by AI is comparable to the transformations that accompanied industrialization, electrification and the internet, each of which required decades of deliberate investment in organizational redesign, workforce development and legal frameworks.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Sets a multi-decade time horizon and historical base rate for AI's real economic payoff, tempering near-term productivity expectations.
insights.locator: We call that neglected other side “reverse alignment”

insights.claim: Complementary institutional investments in workforce and legal frameworks are prerequisites for a technology's success rather than optional supplements, as argued in Carlota Perez's 2002 book on technological revolutions and financial capital.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Grounds the thesis in Perez's technology-surge framework, useful for investors modeling the gap between an installation phase and a deployment/golden-age phase.
insights.quote: History shows that such investments are not optional supplements to technological change; they are prerequisites for its success
insights.locator: as the researcher Carlota Perez argues

insights.claim: A government order forcing Anthropic to cut off access to its two most capable models for all foreign nationals led the company to disable the systems for every customer within hours, illustrating how fast state action can shut down frontier AI access.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: A concrete precedent for abrupt, export-control-style disruption of AI service availability — a tail risk for any business dependent on frontier-model access.
insights.quote: Within hours, the company disabled the systems for every customer.
insights.locator: Last month, the U.S. government ordered Anthropic

insights.claim: AI capabilities are climbing at an exponential rate while policy and social institutions move at their accustomed pace, a mismatch Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei likened to seeking military aid from trees in his June 2026 essay.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Signals that even AI-lab leadership sees institutional lag as the binding constraint, supporting demand for governance and integration tooling.

insights.claim: Anthropic's research division released internal data showing models that have begun to help build their own successors, lending fresh weight to speculation about recursive self-improvement.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: If models materially accelerate their own development, capability timelines compress further, widening the institutional-readiness gap the authors warn about.
insights.quote: models that have begun to help build their own successors
insights.locator: The same week, the company’s research division

themes.theme: Three Failure Modes of Institutional Neglect

insights.claim: General-purpose technologies create broad value only after heavy complementary investment in new processes, skills and organizational redesign; when those complements lag, work is reorganized faster than people can retrain, and gains fail to become security or mobility.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Explains the 'productivity paradox' J-curve; watch complementary-investment lag as the leading indicator of whether AI gains broaden or concentrate.

insights.claim: Once the cost of producing text, images and code collapses, the scarce input becomes human bandwidth to validate outputs, establish provenance and ensure accountability — decisions become easier to automate than to justify.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Points to verification, provenance and accountability tooling as the new scarce resource and a durable demand category as generation costs go to zero.
insights.quote: once the cost of producing text, images and code collapses, the scarce input becomes the human bandwidth to validate outputs, establish provenance and ensure accountability

insights.claim: AI lowers monitoring costs and concentrates discretion, pushing states and large bureaucracies toward centralized control unless contestability, appeal and civil-liberties safeguards advance in tandem.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Frames authoritarian drift as a default outcome absent countervailing infrastructure — a governance risk relevant to civil-liberties tech and public-sector AI oversight.

insights.claim: As the cost of producing convincing fake documents, biometrics and synthetic personas approaches zero, identity verification systems face an escalating crisis of trust, creating urgent need for cryptographic 'personhood credentials' that prove someone is a unique real human without revealing who they are.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Identifies proof-of-personhood as a foundational new market layer; watch W3C DID/VC standards and eIDAS-aligned identity wallet providers.
insights.quote: identity verification systems are facing an escalating crisis of trust

insights.claim: The W3C's Decentralized Identifiers protocol already underpins pilot programs for educational credentialing and healthcare verification under the EU's eIDAS 2.0 framework, making portable personal digital wallets increasingly feasible.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Concrete standards and regulatory rails already exist — a signal of where interoperable digital identity infrastructure is being built first.
insights.locator: Emerging technical standards make this increasingly feasible

insights.claim: Because identity is mostly relational, verifiable-credential architectures could let AI agents themselves be authenticated through social relationships, enabling agentic commerce — for example gaining system access by having two already-trusted friends vouch for you.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Suggests relational/reputation-based identity, not biometrics, as the substrate for the coming agent economy — relevant to agent authentication and trust infrastructure.
insights.locator: someone could gain access to a system by having two friends

insights.claim: AI gives everyone access to a personal 'Sherlock Holmes' that turns scattered digital traces like browsing, location, metadata and CCTV into detailed behavioral profiles at minimal cost, rendering the transparency/anonymity binaries encoded in laws like GDPR archaic and unworkable.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Argues existing privacy law is obsolete and points toward cryptographic 'meronymity' (selective disclosure) as the replacement paradigm.
insights.quote: AI allows everyone access to a personal “Sherlock Holmes,” making scattered digital traces (browsing, location, metadata, CCTV) into detailed behavioral profiles at minimal cost.

insights.claim: Zero-knowledge proofs, secure multiparty computation and federated learning already underpin real activities like social-media age verification, anonymous cryptocurrency transactions and encrypted messaging, enabling computation on data no party can see.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Privacy-preserving computation is already deployed, not theoretical — a maturing toolkit for compliant collaborative AI and confidential data markets.

insights.claim: Deepfakes are often more visually persuasive than real content, increasingly cheap and can be seamlessly woven into accounts of real events to move public opinion, defraud people or undermine morale in countries like Ukraine resisting invasion.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Establishes content provenance as a national-security and anti-fraud imperative, not just a media concern.
insights.quote: Deepfakes, which are often more visually persuasive than real content, are also increasingly cheap

insights.claim: Regulators are already mandating digital provenance: the EU AI Act requires disclosure of realistic AI-generated content, California's AI Transparency Act requires detectable watermarking, and China's Deep Synthesis Provisions require signed consent from anyone imitated.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Three major jurisdictions already legislate provenance/watermarking, creating compliance demand and tailwinds for content-credential vendors.

insights.claim: The Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity's content-credentials standard, paired with imperceptible watermarking, enables high-confidence authentication of whether content is AI or human, and tools like VeriTrail extend provenance into AI reasoning to trace claims back to source materials.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
entities: Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity
insights.so_what: Names specific provenance standards and tooling to track as authentication infrastructure builds out.

insights.claim: Society's understanding of media may need to shift from assuming photos, videos and voice recordings are direct representations of reality toward treating them like paintings, whose truth value depends on trusting the people who vouch for their authenticity.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: A cultural-epistemic shift back to trust-based verification of media has second-order implications for journalism, courts, insurance and evidence.
insights.locator: Understandings of provenance may also need to shift

insights.claim: AI companies face a coming scarcity because the next rounds of training demand fresh, high-quality human-generated data and the easy supply has been exhausted, while content creators are stuck choosing between modest one-off licensing deals or copyright lawsuits.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Frames training-data scarcity and creator compensation as a central AI-era economic dispute — a value pool up for grabs between labs and rights holders.
insights.quote: The next rounds of training will demand fresh, high-quality human-generated data, and the easy supply has been exhausted.

insights.claim: A real market in training data is feasible because AI companies already produce the two datasets required for pricing every time they train: data-mixture decisions reveal how to divide the value, and scaling laws reveal how much additional data is worth.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Rebuts the 'data can't be priced' objection, implying royalty-style data markets are technically tractable — a potential new asset class for content owners.
insights.quote: Data mixture decisions reveal how the contributions of different sources should be weighted, which settles how to divide the pie. Scaling laws reveal how much additional data improves a model, which settles the size of the pie.
insights.locator: That objection does not survive scrutiny

insights.claim: Music-industry collective management organizations that license works and pay royalties to many rights holders without per-transaction negotiation offer a template for routing AI training-data compensation to creators at scale.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Points to a proven institutional model (CMOs/royalty pools) as the likely mechanism for scaled data compensation, and notes markets for later-stage data (steering, retrieval) via players like Scale AI are already emerging.

insights.claim: Today's AI agents are built to be unconditionally helpful, making them dangerously naive collaborators liable to be tricked by hackers into divulging their principals' confidential data.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Highlights agent trust and prompt-injection defense as a critical unmet need before multi-agent collaboration can scale safely.
insights.quote: today’s agents are built to be unconditionally helpful, making them dangerously naive partners

insights.claim: Without shared cross-provider trust standards, AI agents will only cooperate within walled gardens, letting incumbent platforms capture most of the value from agentic collaboration.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Interoperable agent-trust standards are the battleground that determines whether value concentrates in incumbents or spreads — a key platform-strategy watch item.
insights.quote: Without shared standards, agents will cooperate only within walled gardens, letting incumbent platforms capture most of the value.
insights.locator: Crucially, these trust systems must work across AI model providers

insights.claim: Most platform designs fuel 'pluralistic ignorance at scale' by serving content based on inferred group memberships while hiding why content is served, preventing users from knowing which communities see and assent to the same material.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Diagnoses a structural flaw in attention-based platforms and motivates community-coherence business models as an alternative.

insights.claim: When platform business models shift so communities pay for coherence rather than advertisers paying for attention, platform incentives align with the social fabric they depend on.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Proposes an alternative to the ad-attention model — a potential disruption vector for social-platform monetization.
insights.quote: When the business model shifts so that communities pay platforms for coherence rather than advertisers for attention, platform incentives align with the social fabric they depend on.
insights.locator: communal models can present issues from the vantage points

themes.theme: Historical Precedents and Proven Civic Tech

insights.claim: India Stack gave hundreds of millions of unbanked people financial access via Aadhaar (biometric ID for over 1.3 billion), UPI and DigiLocker, but Aadhaar authentication failures have denied food rations, pensions and welfare to low-caste, tribal and migrant communities, showing technical architecture and political governance are inseparable.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: A cautionary case that centralized biometric ID is dual-use surveillance infrastructure whose risk depends on the administering state's democratic health.
insights.quote: The same system that achieved financial inclusion at extraordinary speed also created new vectors of exclusion and control

insights.claim: X's Community Notes, adopted in 2021 and since copied by Meta and TikTok, are trusted across the political spectrum and roughly halve resharing of the posts they annotate, extending Taiwan's Pol.is 'bridging statement' logic to platform scale.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that bridging-based moderation works at scale and is spreading across major platforms — a template for AI-era information governance.
insights.quote: trusted across the political spectrum and roughly halve resharing of the posts they annotate
insights.locator: The same bridging logic now runs at platform scale

insights.claim: The Bretton Woods institutions (IMF, World Bank, GATT) supplied the scaffolding for the postwar 'thirty glorious years' of 1945-1975, the fastest sustained economic growth in history, showing purpose-built institutions can unlock long booms.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Historical base rate that deliberate institution-building can precede and enable multi-decade growth surges — the outcome the authors want to replicate for AI.

insights.claim: AI dramatically lowers the cost of surveillance, investigation, regulatory drafting and enforcement, and these capabilities accrue disproportionately to the executive branch, shifting power away from the legislature and judiciary toward a unilateral executive.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Warns that AI structurally advantages executive power; countervailing tooling (audit trails, public legal models) becomes a democratic-resilience investment area.

insights.claim: Many frictions AI dissolves — the effort to build a case, the practical limits on simultaneous investigations — have functioned as constitutional safeguards that screen out meritless or retaliatory state actions before they can scale.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Reframes bureaucratic 'friction' as a feature; removing it without new checks is a civil-liberties risk to monitor as governments adopt AI.
insights.locator: Many of the frictions AI dissolves

insights.claim: Japan's fastest-growing political party, Team Mirai, channels citizen input through always-on AI-assisted deliberation that translates lived experience into concrete policy proposals in near real time.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: A live example of AI-native democratic participation gaining real electoral traction — signal that civic AI can scale politically.

insights.claim: Legislatures must rebuild analytical capacity eroded since the 1995 defunding of the U.S. Congress's Office of Technology Assessment, or they will cede the design of AI-driven enforcement to the executive by default.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Identifies legislative technical capacity as a specific, addressable gap in AI governance.

themes.theme: Organizations, Labor Transition and Knowledge Work

insights.claim: The true bottleneck to massive AI productivity gains is transforming organizations to process massively increased creativity and agency, not simply inserting models into existing structures.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Implies the winners will be organizations that redesign workflows around AI's flexibility, analogous to factories rebuilt around electric motors rather than just swapping engines.
insights.quote: The true bottleneck to massive productivity gains from AI is transforming organizations to process massively increased creativity and agency.

insights.claim: AI unbundles professional expertise into discrete tasks, destabilizing an industrial-era workforce stack of unemployment insurance, workforce boards and community colleges built for rigid job descriptions and mass single-employer layoffs.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Legacy labor-transition infrastructure is mismatched to continuous task reallocation — a policy failure risk and a market for portable-credential and task-mapping tools.

insights.claim: Model developers possess high-resolution real-time signals of how human work is actually being reshaped, but the state lacks the data-sharing frameworks to translate those signals into responsive training or portable credentials.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Names an actionable information asymmetry between AI labs and government — data-sharing frameworks are a concrete gap to close for labor policy.
insights.quote: Model developers possess high-resolution signals regarding how human work is actually being reshaped, yet the state and its critical partners lack the data-sharing frameworks
insights.locator: The legacy infrastructure of unemployment insurance

insights.claim: AI now generates hypotheses, runs simulations and drafts manuscripts at a pace that renders traditional peer review functionally obsolete, burying breakthroughs beneath an avalanche of incremental recombination and slop.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Signals coming disruption to scientific publishing and evaluation, with AI-based silo-breaking metrics and 'decentralized science' funding as emerging responses.
insights.quote: Models now generate hypotheses, run simulations and draft manuscripts at a pace that renders traditional peer review functionally obsolete

insights.claim: AI breaks the university's implicit bundling of skills with content delivery, forcing educators to make explicit which capacities matter and to use AI as a 'stationary bike for the mind' rather than the effortless 'e-bike' of Sam Altman's metaphor.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI-in-education debate around deliberate skill-building versus effortless output — relevant to edtech design and credentialing reform.
insights.quote: using AI as a stationary bike for the mind, not merely the “e-bike” of OpenAI CEO Sam Altman’s metaphor

insights.claim: Multisectoral 'smart cities' coalitions of a decade ago mostly failed to deliver because experts from different fields disagreed on what problem they were solving, a cautionary precedent for AI-era sociotechnical coalitions.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Warns that cross-sector AI initiatives fail on coordination, not ideas — execution and coalition design, not technology, is the binding constraint.
insights.quote: When experts from different fields converge on a novel challenge, they often discover they disagree on what problem they are trying to solve
insights.locator: Just over a decade ago, multisectoral coalitions

insights.claim: Advance market commitments, where funders guarantee demand for products that do not yet exist, were transformative for Operation Warp Speed's Covid-19 vaccine and could similarly de-risk building sociotechnical infrastructure like interoperable credentialing or privacy-preserving computation.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Names a concrete financing mechanism (AMCs) that philanthropists and governments could deploy to bootstrap AI-integration infrastructure.

insights.claim: The binding constraint on societal AI adaptation is rarely the absence of good ideas but the absence of institutional arrangements and patient capital willing to sustain them over the long period before results compound.
insights.speaker: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
insights.so_what: Points to long-horizon 'patient capital' and institutional design — not R&D — as the scarce resource for capturing AI's broad benefits.
insights.quote: the binding constraint is rarely the absence of good ideas but the absence of institutional arrangements that let different actors contribute what only they can, and the patient capital willing to sustain those arrangements
insights.locator: Reverse alignment is not a single project

source_meta.author: E. GLEN WEYL, JAMES EVANS AND CHRIS WHITE
